data.title: The Market Is Giving You A Gift, Don't Overthink It
data.description:
It has been a little over a month since I wrote the article “Not A Bear Market Yet, But It’s Already A Stock Picker’s Dream.” My opinion on the market in general was simple: the main indexes had already undergone a certain correction, but the real opportunity was in stock picking.

Now, I think the scenario has already changed. I am still a stock picker, and I focus on that. But the market in general is very undervalued. Looking at indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, you are buying excellent companies at valuations that are already very interesting.

I don't think it's something as generalized as “buy anything and it will go up,” but on average, speaking of indexes and good ETFs, I am already much more optimistic, and the scenario seems to me to be the type of case that is rare to find.

S&P 500 And Nasdaq: Quality Companies With Reasonable Valuation

Since I am a stock picker and a fundamental analyst, I will start by talking about what you are buying when you buy US Indexes.

Everyone knows that when you buy an S&P 500 (SP500) ETF like SPY or IVV, you are buying the 500 largest American companies.

But more specifically, you have a higher weight for gigantic companies. Not surprisingly, Nvidia (NVDA) is 7% of the entire index, Apple (AAPL) is 6%, Microsoft (MSFT) is 5%, Amazon (AMZN) is 3.5%, and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) is a little over 5%, adding the two stocks present in the index. The top 10 holdings (SPY) already account for more than ⅓ of the entire index.

This is something that at certain times can be bad, but at other times can be good. In the long run, obviously, it is an allocation and a way of rebalancing the index that was very successful and brought many gains. But at certain times it may seem like “too much” to have 7% of Nvidia or to have ~30% of the portfolio in tech.

But I don't think that is the current moment.

Tech, and especially the Mag 7, have gone through such a challenging moment in recent months that having this focus on tech and mega caps becomes interesting and asymmetric.

I still see some people who are anchored in the thinking from a few years ago that Nvidia is super overvalued and that you have to pay a premium to participate in this growth. This is no longer true.

Note below how the P/E of these companies has decreased.

Now, note how the EPS of these companies has risen.

This is what I am talking about. Of course, you still have the other ~70% composed of other companies from different sectors, where some are at a fair valuation, others overvalued, and others even cheaper than the mega caps. But in general, this is already a proxy to say that the main companies in the index are not doing poorly; they are improving, and meanwhile, multiples are compressing.

Adding these two factors, we have a better margin of safety for the main companies in the index, and as a “bonus,” you still get good diversification in case the market is right about hyperscalers being a riskier choice now, you possess commodities in case there is a commodity supercycle, and so on.

By the way, bringing an anecdote to the article, I don't remember the last time I felt this way. I am cautious in my purchases and new additions to the portfolio, with a lot of patience. Now I feel that there are so many opportunities at once that it is even difficult to avoid "over diversifying" the portfolio. Microsoft, for instance, is a company that I still don't have in my portfolio, but at this price I want to have it and start a position; recently I did this with Meta (META), and I am also increasing my position in Amazon. So there are so many attractive options that it seems difficult even to choose (and this is something an ETF could help with).

Why This Is The Best Time To Buy

Moving to more technical and momentum factors, to prove that this is a rare momentum, note in the chart below how both QQQ and SPY have very rarely been below their exponential moving average (200) in the last 10 years.

For both the Nasdaq and the S&P 500, the last time was during the sharp correction at the beginning of 2025 and also in the bear market of 2022 if we disregard other dips that were fast and corrected in a short time.

For me, combining the EMA (200) with the qualitative part of the S&P 500 is already enough to defend a buy. Buying quality companies, arguably the best in the world, below a historical average is important.

Besides that, the P/E is another important point. It is not something exclusive to mega caps. Even taking a long history, such as 10 years, the QQQ P/E is well below the average. 21.5x for the next 12 months against an average of 24.5x. For the S&P 500, the average is 19.7x, and the P/E is already at 19.8x. And this includes some outliers, like the dip in 2019, the pandemic crash, etc.

A Word About Cycles And Time Arbitrage

But of course, all this that I mentioned does not guarantee that the investor will have returns in the short term. Even being below the 200-day average, even having an attractive P/E, and even having many quality companies and compounders, the next quarters could be even worse.

Let's assume that, for whatever reason, AI is something that will not yield results. Data centers and electricity become expensive to the point of making AI agents unfeasible, or AI agents bring neither a revenue increase nor a margin increase for the companies that are investing in this. Truly the index would deserve to fall and go through a worse bear market than what we are seeing. This is because this affects not only hyperscalers like Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet, but it also affects the expectations of several companies in the index.

And if the estimates are wrong, then the forward P/E is also wrong. Think, for example, that if Alphabet's net income is 10% lower in a 20-year flow, that is more or less the correction Alphabet has to have. But in fact, the level of correction could be even worse, because it is not just shareholder value that matters to the market; there is also the issue of the discount rate and safety. Perhaps the market still sees a total return of ~10% for the main tech stocks but doesn't think it is “enough” for the uncertainties and then prefers to have a total return of ~5% in Walmart (WMT) or Costco (COST) (rough assumptions just to prove my point).

These are uncertainties that deserve to be considered. But I am not a short-term trader, and therefore, my arguments here are not for a short-term trade. For this reason, I think it's worth talking a little about cycles and time arbitrage.

Recently I wrote an entire article about time arbitrage and how it can be a “superpower.” I highly recommend that you read it here. But in summary, I believe it is possible to have an edge by investing in a timeframe different from the market. Even if the market is right about the fear of AI, CapEx, and the like, and in the next 2 years the free cash flow is lower than the consensus, many of the cases that delivered a poor performance are still intact in the long term.

Microsoft, for example, even if Azure does not grow 40% for a long time, still has a global infrastructure business that is a compounder (at a pace close to double digits), and this comes with tons of optionalities that could materialize in the next 10 years. This is also true for Amazon, Alphabet, and several others in the index.

So buying the S&P 500, which is full of the best companies in the world at a reasonable valuation with a “good enough” expected growth, is already more than enough to have good returns, as long as you wait long enough and are willing to endure the volatility (and that is where factors like asset allocation and having fixed income, among others, come in).

Besides that, I think it's important to talk about cyclicality. In recent years we saw a cycle very favorable to the US and specifically to tech stocks. Consequently, I see many people saying that now is the time to invest in international stocks, emerging markets, etc.

I don't totally disagree with this view. There are opportunities in emerging markets. And being Brazilian, I know that if there is a commodity supercycle, the Brazilian index will do very well (and possibly will outperform the S&P 500). This is because it is still a stock market and a domestic economy with significant exposure to oil, iron ore, agricultural commodities, etc.

But going back to the subject of US equities, I think many let something go unnoticed. In the 2000s, if Brazil did very well, that mattered little to the main US companies. Maybe one or two would benefit together, etc.

Nowadays it is not like that. Microsoft is a global company, Alphabet is a global company, and Meta is a global company. All these “tech stocks,” in fact, have exposure to most world economies. If Latin America delivers an economic boom in the coming years, that means a population more willing to spend, companies with more demand for ads, and ads that perform better, and this also affects tech stocks in a certain way.

Illustrating this, Netflix (NFLX) had $5.3 billion in revenue in the UCAN region (US and Canada) in Q4. But in Latin America (LATAM), the revenue is $1.4 billion. Asia Pacific (APAC) is also $1.4 billion. Joining these emerging markets, we have an extremely important revenue line impacted by currency and by domestic economies, which, in a good cycle for emerging markets, also affects Netflix. Of course, I am not saying that these two revenues will be able to “save” the stock in an American recession. What I am saying is that if US revenue growth stops growing at 18% and starts growing at 8%, but APAC and LATAM continue growing at ~20% (or even accelerate), Netflix remains a growth company.

In other words, even though it is heavily tech-focused, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq not only remain quite solid; in fact, they are even stronger now, with companies with a moat wider than 20 years ago.

Bottom Line

In short, I think in a few weeks we went from “we have to be cautious in selecting stocks” to “the US market is appealing” in general. As I mentioned throughout the article, I don't think the momentum is risk-free; there truly are uncertainties. But looking at it as a long-term investor, the US market offers quality compounders for a reasonable (or cheap) valuation.

data.title: 3 Market Predictions For April
data.description:
Here we are on the last day of March as the first quarter comes to a merciful end for investors, albeit a nice rally took place Tuesday morning. We are coming to the close of the quarter with both the Dow and NASDAQ in official correction territory, while the S&P 500 is off roughly 9% from its recent highs as of market close Monday. Not surprisingly, the energy sector was the only one of the 11 sectors in the S&P that posted a gain in March.

Brent oil had a record surge for a month in March, and the WTI oil benchmark crossed the $100/barrel threshold for the first time since 2022. So, what is ahead for investors in the first month of the second quarter? Three market predictions for April follow below.

Extreme Oil Volatility

Obviously, this appears to be an easy call. Brent crude moved above $110/barrel late this month for the first time since 2022. Oil's direction seems very binary right now. Brent will either end April at $135/barrel or above and push global economies to the brink of recession or will fall under $85/barrel, and global investors will breathe a huge sigh of relief.

And it all depends on which scenario plays out in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz. Obviously, other key commodities and energy products are highly dependent on this situation in April. These include jet fuel, diesel, gasoline, and LNG on the energy side and key ingredients for fertilizer as well as aluminum, helium, and a variety of other key compounds.

If the Strait of Hormuz is forced open this month either by a resolution to this conflict or by military force, oil should plunge back to the $85/barrel level or so on Brent. That would be above the mid- to high-$70s it was trading at prior to this regional war but still far below current levels. The equity market should see a significant bounce if things play out this week in April. Whether that rebound is sustainable given other key issues right now is less certain.

It should be noted that even if the Strait of Hormuz opened this week, it is going to take time to recover from this massive disruption. Smelters, refineries, and chemical plants take time to restart. Global supply chains will take many weeks or months to recover. Oil reserves that have been released in the U.S. and Europe will need to be rebuilt over time. And global economies will still see an uptick in inflation due to higher food, commodity, and chemical costs working their way through the logistical ecosystem. This could be offset somewhat by demand destruction. That said, economic disaster would have been averted if this global chokepoint reopened for business.

If at this time next month, the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively closed, it is hard to see how oil is not at least $135/barrel or higher. Others see oil moving to $150 to $200 a barrel in this scenario. Asia will be the first to face significant fuel shortages. China gets 35% of its oil supplies from this passage, including 15% from Iran. That said, the country does have 4 months of oil reserves in storage. Other countries like India, Japan, and Australia are not in as strong a position. Europe will see fuel shortages soon after Asia.

Higher gasoline, jet fuel, diesel, and fertilizer prices will have significant impacts on the U.S. economy and perhaps tilt it into recession, depending on how long this conflict lasts. Gas prices at the pump topped four bucks a gallon for the first time since August 2022 this week. It should be noted that the U.S. only gets 7% of its oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. And Venezuela is now a bigger potential supplier to the U.S. given recent events in that country.

The Private Credit Pandora's Box Will Not Close In April

If it weren't for events in Iran, the fast deterioration in the private credit markets would be front and center as far as investors' key concerns around the market. Problems at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in late February reignited fears around the private credit markets, which have seen huge growth since the Great Financial Crisis.

The Pandora's Box that is private credit got opened significantly wider in March. In the second week of the month, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) reduced the value of some loans ‌to private credit funds. This happened after the bank reviewed the impact of market turmoil around software companies, which make up a good chunk of private credits' loan book. This sent some shockwaves across the space. And growing fears of private credit contagion was one of my three predictions for March, it should be noted.

During March, there was a cacophony of large private credit funds at major players in the space that 'gated' quarterly redemption requests. These names included some of the largest funds in the industry, including BlackRock (BLK), Apollo Global Management (APO), Cliffwater, Morgan Stanley (MS), and Ares Management Corporation (ARES). Generally, investors got roughly half of the funds requested for the quarter.

Redemption requests were already moving up significantly in recent quarters at major funds. Given this quarter's gating, redemption requests in the coming quarter are likely heading significantly higher. The horses seem like they have escaped from the barn, and investors should expect more negative headlines around private credit in April. The shares of alternative investment managers are already deep in bear market territory and feel more like 'value traps' than buy-the-dip opportunities.

Gold Will Rebound

Speaking of bear market territory, gold briefly went there in March. There are several reasons the yellow metal headed down in March. The precious metal had a huge run over 2023-2025 and kind of went parabolic early this year along with silver.

In addition, the spike in interest rates during March provided a headwind for precious metals as well. This helped the dollar to post its strongest month since 2022, which helped push gold down as well during the month. There were also some significant gold sales from central banks during the month, including 60 tons from Turkey as it defends the Turkish lira. Russia has been a significant seller of gold in 2026 as well to shore up its finances as the war in Ukraine hit its fifth year in February. Higher oil and energy prices due to the conflict in the Middle East, as well as the removal of some energy sanctions, have alleviated that need, it should be noted.

Goldman Sachs was out this week reiterating they expect gold to rise to $5,400 an ounce by the end of 2026. Wells Fargo sees gold in a range of $6,100 to $6,300 an ounce by year. BMO Capital recently raised its year-end target for gold as well. Given the continuing and growing worries around fiat currencies and unsustainable sovereign debt throughout most of the G-20, the blip down in March in gold prices feels like it could have been a 'hiccup' after a long run-up.

Gold feels like it should get a decent rebound in April after big losses in March. If the Strait of Hormuz reopens in April, I will likely add to my covered call holdings in names like Newmont Mining (NEM). Gold miners should benefit as gold starts to rally again. If oil plunges, miners will doubly benefit, given energy prices make up a good chunk of their operational costs.

And those are some key ways I see the markets playing out in April. Investors faced more uncertainty to start the second quarter than at any time since volatility spiked in early April of last year when reciprocal tariffs were announced. This news triggered a huge downturn in the markets over the next week and sent the S&P 500 down 18% intraday before many of these tariff policies were 'walked back'. Are we facing a similar situation as the first quarter comes to a close? We will find out together in April.

data.title: Stocks Plunged In March, But Hope Emerges In The U.S.-Iran War
data.description:
Stocks plummeted in March as the war in Iran sent oil prices (CO1:COM) soaring, but geopolitical rays of hope emerged to close the quarter. The S&P 500 ETF (SP500, SPY) fell more than 5%, nearly its worst month since September 2022. Despite a powerful 'Turnaround Tuesday' to finish the first quarter, it was red across the board, away from Energy. The Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) shed 5%, very close to the declines in mid-caps (MDY) and the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM).

Foreign stocks were hit even harder. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) slid 9%, with emerging markets feeling the brunt of higher Brent and WTI crude oil prices.

In the bond market, the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury rate climbed 34 basis points to 4.30%. The positive stock-bond correlation was negative news for the iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), which suffered a 2% March decline (dividends included).

Global oil obviously took off, resulting in a 53% monthly increase in the United States Oil Fund (USO)--its best month on record (even with a loss on the 31st). Oil’s gain contrasted with a massive 12% decline in gold (GLD). The yellow metal had a hot start to the year but is now down nearly 20% from its January all-time high. Finally, bitcoin (IBIT) perhaps surprisingly rallied 3% in March, all while the US Dollar ETF (UUP) added 3%.

Oil's March Jolt Stunned Stocks

Digging into the sector view, Energy (XLE) was the big winner—no surprises there. Up more than 10% for March, oil & gas equities lagged physical oil in a major way. Still, XLE’s rise looked very strong compared to the 10 other S&P 500 sector ETFs. Worst among them was Industrials (XLI), which lost 9%. Steam was let out of the AI trade to an extent, while profit-taking in the Aerospace & Defense industry stymied XLI.

Interestingly, defensive sectors failed to provide safety. Utilities (XLU) was "least bad," down 4%. But Real Estate (XLRE), Health Care (XLV), and Staples (XLP) all endured sharp March losses. And the growth trade was also weak—tech-related companies saw their shares get re-rated. The likes of Nvidia (NVDA), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) now sport P/E ratios that are heavily discounted relative to recent years.

March S&P 500 Sector ETF Performance

U.S. Stocks Stumbled in March, Energy Shares Rose

Massive March Oil & Gas Gains, Precious Metals Plummet

Turning to the economy, March began like a lion—both geopolitically and regarding the data. The February jobs report was much worse than expected, with a 92,000 reported employment decline and a notable uptick in the jobless rate. Average hourly earnings remain fine, and we’ll get a fresh slate of March labor-market reports beginning on Wednesday.

Keep in mind that the official payrolls update hits on Good Friday (a market holiday).

U.S. Labor Market Remains Weak

All eyes will be on the consumer this week and throughout April. The now-dated February Retail Sales report hits on April 1, and there’s no fooling the reality that those numbers won’t capture now much-higher gasoline prices.

On April 30, the price for a gallon of regular unleaded topped $4 for the first time since 2022, up a dollar from the close of February. The data we do have from March suggests no meaningful consumer slowdown yet.

U.S. Retail Sales Still Decent... Before the Oil Spike

Market-watchers will also center on CPI inflation data later in April. As of February, the YoY headline CPI rate was a somewhat tame 2.4%. That’s set to change meaningfully, thanks to the eye-popping surge in oil prices. U.S. inflation could rise above 3% YoY in short order, but the expectation is that WTI and RBOB gasoline will drift lower as 2026 progresses (at least according to the respective futures curves).

The good news is that the economy will begin lapping increased tariff rates on the 12-month lookback.

U.S. Inflation Rate Poised To Tick Up

U.S. Gasoline > $4/Gallon

As for the Fed, outgoing Chair Powell offered relief to markets on March’s penultimate day. In a fireside chat with Harvard University students, the Fed chief downplayed the chance of a rate hike.

Indeed, the Fed Funds futures market moved. There’s now a scant market-implied chance of a quarter-point tightening between now and year-end. Before the speech, futures priced in close to an 80% probability of a hike. Further de-escalation rhetoric between the U.S. and Iran helped squash those chances on the final trading day of the month, too.

Fed Expected to Hold Its Policy Rate

Ironically, U.S. real GDP growth expectations have been relatively stable. Prediction markets peg the inflation-adjusted Q1 pace of economic expansion at 2.6%.

That’s down a bit from a month ago, but it’s really not much damage, all told.

Still-Solid Q1 U.S. Real GDP Seen

What’s not turning worse? S&P 500 earnings per share estimates. According to FactSet, both CY 2026 and CY 2027 EPS forecasts are on the rise. Couple the drastic S&P 500 loss in March with rising profit expectations, and the market’s price-to-earnings multiple has compressed massively.

The U.S. large-cap index sported a teens P/E before the solid March 31 rally.

S&P 500 EPS Estimates on the Rise

Looking ahead, April has historically delivered solid returns. The average S&P 500 gain is 1.1% from 2010 to 2025, but the last two instances have been negative.

We’ll see if the 2025 playbook continues to unfold—it's two straight years of an up November, down December, up January, and down February & March. The bulls would certainly take that trade, given the major May-September climbs in 2024 and 2025.

S&P 500: Bullish Seasonality Ahead

The Bottom Line

Stocks plunged in March, but glimmers of hope came about on the final day of the month. Global equities suffered the largest monthly loss since September 2022 as the war in Iran caused oil prices to skyrocket. Geopolitical uncertainty remains very high heading into April, with macro eyes on the Strait of Hormuz.

Still, economists will parse key data ahead, as investors and traders monitor price action. The S&P 500 came very close to technical correction territory toward the end of March. The war, AI’s impact, private credit, and a soft labor market remain headwinds.

data.title: How Investors Are Navigating Market Turmoil
data.description:
Markets have been rattled by escalating Middle East tensions. Jeff Evans, VP, Director and Lead for Empirical Research and PM Support with TD Asset Management, breaks down how investors are shifting to defensive and quality stocks in a search for stability.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Markets have been volatile as the Middle East conflict continues, but under the surface, there are some shifts taking place. Joining us now to discuss is Jeff Evans from TD Asset Management. Jeff, great to have you back. Everyone is trying to navigate this market and figure out what's going on. Let's talk about the implications of the conflict.

Jeff Evans: First of all, great to be back. Always a pleasure to be here. Thanks for having me on. And yeah, it's been not just one year, but multiple years now of volatility in the markets. This, of course, just being the latest incident to come in. We've had this now for the past year with a lot of tariff turmoil and other geopolitical news. Take it back the last five years, we've had inflation, interest rates, COVID. Seems to be just one shock after another hitting the markets.

I think when you get into these periods of volatility and uncertainty, the important thing is to take a step back and look at things at a bigger picture level. And for me, that is, what drives markets over the longer term? Ultimately, it's earnings. If earnings go up, it may not happen right away, but eventually, the market tends to recognize that. You do tend to see stock prices follow earnings over the long term. It's a very important anchor.

And so really the key here is, let's just take a step back and see what's happening with earnings growth and where earnings growth still is in the market. Of course, over the past 10 to 15 years, it's really been about the US and the US tech stocks. That's been the dominant source of earnings growth.

What you started to see last year was interesting. It was the first year in really 15 years where earnings growth started to broaden outside of the United States. EAFE hit an all-time record high. It took 15 years to get there, but all-time record high. Started to see some earnings growth and other opportunities that weren't just US tech companies. Lots of people started to expand outside of the US and go to other markets.

And then what you've had now is this other conflict come in that is now questioning whether we need to be rotating. So the first thing is people are going back and saying, well, hey, wait a minute. The US is also at record high earnings. It's not just EAFE. It's still the US. And the US is still growing earnings at a faster rate than EAFE.

So yeah, it's great. EAFE's got a record earnings level. They're growing earnings, but I can still get better in the United States. And I don't have to deal with all the conflict, the uncertainty, and everything else.

The other key piece here is, I think people are reminded a little bit about how fragile growth is outside of the United States. Just to give you a couple of examples of that, if you look at Australia-- and I was reading about this earlier today-- Australia is down to two or three refineries to serve the entire continent.

It's a little frightening. They've really unwound a lot of that capacity over time. Heavily dependent. 90% plus of gasoline imports into Australia are seaborne. They come from other countries where they refine the crude. So you have two to three ships a day coming into Australia. If those ships stop showing up, you've got about 30 days of supply of gasoline for the entire country.

I don't want to think about what happens if that disappears, but you have very, very fragile growth in Australia. That's maybe one of the more severe examples out there. But you're seeing the same thing across Europe. We saw this with the Russia crisis a few years ago. Gas prices go up, or you couldn't even get access to the gas. German industrial capacity collapsed 25% to 30% in a very short period of time. And it's either because it just wasn't economic to run the plants, or you're just going to get the gas to run them. And so when you have an event like this, it just reminds everybody.

Unfortunately-- in Europe's case, they fixed a little bit of the supply issue. But then you've also got cases like Australia, where they've actually just made their scenario worse over time and really haven't focused on fixing the supply chain.

What that all brings it back to is if you're looking for robust earnings growth, unfortunately, it's the US. And so what we're seeing in a lot of our empirical research models, if you look at this from a factor lens, just kind of what's working on a style basis, it's quality. And in particular, profitable companies.

We can debate whether the US dollar is a currency hedge that it used to be in the past. We can debate whether the US still has the rule of law or institutional strength that did in the past. But what US assets have is a very resilient domestic demand base that just isn't impacted by these supply chain issues. It's a very self-sufficient market. You can continue to grow without disruptions.

You haven't seen-- oil prices are up, but you really haven't seen natural gas spiking. The US economy will be impacted, but on a relative basis. Nowhere near as much. And so a lot of the rotations we've been seeing over the last couple of weeks is into these quality stocks.

And then, of course, the other important thing is you are seeing a bit of a defensive flow. Not surprising at all. When uncertainty spikes up, investors go to safety, low-volatility stocks. We're thinking that may be getting a little bit close to the tail end. That defensive trade is probably 10% to 12% outperformance in a short period of time.

The bigger macro backdrop right now is we're running inflation above target. We have been for about five or six years now-- very different from last decade. That also means interest rates are running at elevated levels.

For low-vol stocks to have sustained outperformance, you need what we had from 1980 to 2019-- falling interest rates, inflation under target. That's just not the environment we're in today. And so we think there's probably maybe a little more room as this uncertainty persists for a few weeks. But ultimately, we'd expect a lot of this defensive rotation to unwind. As the markets settle down, we get a little bit of calm.

But I think importantly, the critical bit here is quality assets are back. And there's a realization that maybe this growth opportunity outside of the US is a little bit more fragile than people thought last year.

Greg Bonnell: One part of the market that obviously had a big move before this conflict erupted in the Middle East was the software stocks, in the sense that when you're talking about rotation of money, perhaps investors getting a little less defensive, looking for quality. What do you think about the software space right now because they've really been beaten down?

Jeff Evans: Yeah. So I think that's the other big rotation we've been seeing over the last little bit. Some people call it the HALO trade. So the Hard Asset, Low Obsolescence stocks moving out of software, which is predominantly intangible. It's basically whatever's in the heads of the engineers, and those engineers can move around whenever they want to.

I think people are realizing there's a value to-- if you have an asset, whether it's real estate, infrastructure, big asset, stuck in the ground, you can't pick up and move it. And as long as it has a very strong business case and a moat, those assets should continue to generate value. So we've certainly seen that rotation in the market.

And then it's just been accelerated by all the advances with AI and Claude that's kind of started at the end of December with just the advances in coding, really questioning whether or not you need programmers anymore. And then that very quickly spread to, well, it's not just programmers. It's insurance agents, and it's maybe real estate brokers and financial analysts. There's all sorts of other questions of how far can AI go?

I think one interesting anecdotal piece, Amazon (AMZN) had a number of disruptions a few weeks ago. They had been relying heavily on AI-generated code. It stopped working. It actually caused the business to slow down in certain cases or even come under pressure. They couldn't run. Bit of a rethink of maybe we need to slow this down and do some more analysis before we just let everything go full AI. And just as one example of maybe we're not completely ready for prime time.

But one of the things that we've been looking at to try and understand this, is we went back to last year. In January of 2025, you had another event around the AI trade, which is DeepSeek. So this was the Chinese research firm that came out and said, we've got a faster way of training these LLMs. We can do it with less memory, less data, almost the same thing as this Google paper last week that's now questioning whether you need all the memory for these things.

What happened when DeepSeek came out was you had a very sharp drop in a lot of these stocks-- 20% to 30% for anybody who's affected within a day or so. And then over the next month or two after that still continued pressure. People kind of questioning the narrative, selling down the names.

And then something interesting happened. It stopped, and things started to stabilize. And it was, again, two to three months. Selling stopped. And then investors started digging in and going, well, maybe not every company is negatively impacted by this. There's probably going to be some winners and losers.

And when we've looked at this closely, as soon as you start seeing positive earnings revisions-- these are names that were down 30%. They start to get some positive earnings revisions. Maybe they're not affected the way the market originally thought. Those names snap back very, very aggressively over the subsequent year, went from down 30% to actually outperforming the market quite significantly.

We're in almost that same time frame now for the software stocks. This has kind of been, depending on when you start measuring, two to three months since a lot of these names have been hit in that initial reaction. And so the question going forward is, are you actually going to start to see that impacting the financials?

Realistically, it's too soon for most of these companies. You're not seeing -- it's not as though this came out, and the next day the business models are broken. But what we're watching for very closely is, are earnings estimates going up? Are these companies adapting? Are they finding ways of telling the market we can grow through this?

And in a lot of cases, if you look at a company like Microsoft (MSFT), share price down 35% from the peak. Earnings estimates are up year to date. They're still finding ways of benefiting from AI. They're a major supplier into that chain through all the hyperscaler AI data centers they're doing.

We're finding the same thing-- again, this made its way all the way into real estate, affecting some of the real estate brokerage firms. But if you look at CBRE (CBRE), Colliers (CIGI), a lot of these firms, the share prices have been hit quite negatively. The earnings estimates continue to go up.

And what a lot of people are realizing is this is not going to be a day one impact. It'll come in gradually over time. And there's actually probably a lot of offsets and good news that can come out of this.

So that's the way we think investors should really look at this. You're now probably through the worst of it. Focus on the fundamentals. And you're going to start to see over the next six to nine months, likely a few winners emerge that as the market starts differentiating, you're going to see some winners and losers.

data.title: The Cure For FOMO With Tech Contrarians
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Sara Awad from Tech Contrarians explains tech dynamics, geopolitical factors and increased investor scrutiny (0:35) Memory and storage: Micron, AMD, Broadcom, Nvidia, Google update (9:20) Energy through the lens of AI (20:00) Super Micro scandal, China chips (23:10)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome back to Investing Experts, Sara Awad from Tech Contrarians, who we've talked to many times, most of the time where tech has been doing very well and investors have been largely bullish. Not very much the case today.

Sara, welcome back to the show. I'd love it if you would catch us up what you're thinking about today as news is ever changing. The tech space is in people's minds, but not in the same way. How are you looking at the markets right now?

Tech Contrarians: So first, thanks for having me back. It's always a great time to talk with you about the tech space and everything happening in general. So great question.

Really, the backdrop on tech, especially after Q1 as we wrap up Q1, is definitely different from the tone that we saw for the bulk of 2025. I think even last time we spoke, which was around mid-February, and that was pre-war, we talked about this dynamic that was building in the market between a tug of war between greed and fear and more weighted towards the latter.

People were kind of afraid, premium valuations kind of. We started seeing big tech companies like Broadcom (AVGO) or like Nvidia (NVDA) report stellar numbers, but the stock still seemed relatively broken. And we saw that continue on and off through Q1. There's definitely a lot more investor scrutiny on the AI cap expense, specifically on the roadmap of where we're heading in this AI cycle.

We saw guys like Credo (CRDO) do exceptionally well. But the stock still is depressed year to date down, I think, about 37%. And so even heading into this war, think that's what a lot of people forget.

Even heading into this war that's really taken up the full of March, the market was on edge, especially when it comes to tech. And so that dynamic between greed and fear only really intensified in the backdrop, I would say. The war really started markets.

At the beginning, investors acted relatively complacent. And I think that this was an extent of FOMO or a conditioning because of what we saw last April with the April Liberation Day from Trump. The whole market melted. And then a couple of days later, we had a buy signal from a Truth Social post and then a 90-day pause and everything really rallied from there.

So everyone's kind of worried about missing out seemingly on that aha moment, right, on that comeback. And that's why I think markets are so caught up on Trump's every Truth Social post, right? And so we're seeing this kind of dynamic play out.

And at the same time, it's interesting because just as investors are kind of watching how this work plays out, watching for a signal of resolution from Trump, think Trump, the way we're looking at it, Trump is also very much in tune to how financial markets are pricing this in.

And so if we look back at the totality of March, every time we saw the market really begin to price in the gravity of the situation and it is a potentially catastrophic situation if we don't get a resolution sooner, if we see this dragged on further.

Every time we saw the market really begin to price in the gravity of that, we had a post from Trump kind of signal that things are going well or that this is going to be resolved soon. So we saw this on March 9th, markets began to price in the heat. S&P (SP500) was falling, that was falling before Trump came out and he said, if you remember, the war is very complete, pretty much, and kind of gave that kind of mini relief rally.

Again, we saw this on the 24th of March. then again, we saw this. So that was, we saw that last Monday, we saw this this Monday, and we were over each and every time of this, I think the dynamic that's showing up is that the relief rallies are more and more kind of losing steam. So there's less faith about a resolution to this sooner.

And I think that especially that turned around last week when we saw the Iran side really kind of deny potential talks that are happening towards a resolution. And that was really the card that Trump was pointing forward in these posts.

It takes two to tango at the end of the day. So the counterpart is denying talks. I think that came as a huge kind of red flag for the markets. And that's why we saw it continue through Thursday, even after Trump extended that deadline until April 6. And then we saw that really continuing to yesterday.

And then today we're seeing things turn around a bit with the most recent update that Iran is also ready to perhaps put an end to this if there's given specific guarantees. And this comes in light of the Wall Street Journal saying that Trump said that the war could potentially be resolved even without the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. So there's a lot of different moving parts.

I think we've seen the weight of this war and where the importance of it shift a lot throughout this conversation. But I think one thing remains true. If we think about this in terms of takeaway, that the market trusts Trump, even if it's against a better judgment. Everyone has that overarching FOMO fear. No one wants to miss out on the rally that comes out of the resolution of this war.

And simultaneously, Trump, I think, is very much, specifically this administration in tune to how financial markets are reacting and pricing in the current situation. And I think that's why we're seeing kind of a pullback on the escalation of the situation from the US's side, at least.

Rena Sherbill: We talk about context a lot here, I guess it's just ever more important every day. Context is important. So appreciate you highlighting those notes. How are you digesting this news, like news of a conflict resolution, various factors in the geopolitical realm? How do you digest just that part of things?

Tech Contrarians: So when we look at it, of course, we're tech analysts first and foremost. So we look at this and we look at a potential resolution on the table.

And I think that's a massive relief to a prolonged conflict that would really pressure specifically players in Asia like SK Hynix (HXSCL), like Samsung (SSNLF), like TSMC (TSM), especially when it comes to helium.

I think that was a core issue in terms of the supply chain disruption given that Qatar really produces a third of the global helium supply.

And that's used in cooling wafers during etching. It's also used in flushing out toxic residue. So Samsung and SK Hynix were the most exposed here, considering that South Korea imports something like 65 % of its helium from Qatar. And then you have TSMC and (UMC) were also exposed, but just a lesser extent because they recycle their helium at rates of 60 to 70%.

They still need to get replenished daily from imports, but they were exposed to a lesser extent. So to see this resolved, I think that really takes the weight off of what is a core in the semiconductor supply chain, which is TSMC, SK Hynix, Samsung, especially given the ballooning demand for chips and the ballooning demands for HBM memory, considering this huge AI infrastructure build out that remains underway, although stocks aren't necessarily moving like it.

And so that's, that's the first part that we take a look at. And then the second part is that there are two impacts from this if we look at this direct and indirect impact. The first is what you feel at the pump.

The second and the more scary one to us is how this trickles into higher costs for shipping, higher costs for transport, and overall worsening inflation. And that would be really catastrophic for markets.

Already we've seen the expectations we had for Fed rate cuts this year winnowed down from where they were at the beginning of the year.

So I think overall we're looking at a healthier kind of 2026 if we see an end to this sooner rather than later. I think that the fact that earlier today we had a signal from the Iran side that there's a potential resolution on the table as a green flag that was missing for the past week when we got news of productive conversations but nothing from the other side, the counterpart.

So I think that things now are shaping up towards a resolution on a more solid front because we have both sides confirming this is underway and we also know that Trump has really taken a step back from the commitment to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

It seems that he's more using that as a leverage point against Europe being kind of taking a step back, removing his hands and saying that the US is okay with its oil supply, they're doing all right, and that Europe and the rest of the world can go deal with this.

I think that it's turned more political and global on that front. It's not isolated to just the region anymore. And I think that seeing Trump pull out of the war situation rather than escalating it is, I think it's the smartest thing to do. And I think the fact that we're seeing this kind of language come out of the administration really does point to the fact that there was potentially miscalculation going into this to begin with.

Because if we have the Strait of Hormuz blocked and a potential on the table for the Bab al-Mandeeb Strait to be closed as well, then we're looking at a blockage of 30% of the global container shipments and 22% of the global oil supply. So that would really be catastrophic.

So I think we're seeing green signals on this pulling back both for the tech world and for the macro backdrop in general.

Rena Sherbill: In terms of updating, just in terms of your last episode, which was in the middle of February, and you were you were highlighting things that have come to pass, such as Micron (MU) at that time was surging. And you were talking about needing to be cautious based on how much upside was left there and what was going to happen.

Also talking about some semi players offering some strong upside. You've continuously talked about (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO) and Nvidia (NVDA). How would you update investors on those names in particular, in those subsectors of the tech sector?

Tech Contrarians: I'm really happy to revisit those because a lot's happened. I know it's been a little over a month, but it's been definitely eventful, especially when it comes to memory. So we've had these memory players, Micron, even storage guys, Seagate (STX), Western Digital (WDC), really go on a rally.

They've been the outperformers last quarter and this quarter as well. And about two weeks ago now, we had Micron report and there was a stellar report by all metrics, they did extremely well. And heading into that report, the stock had broken its resistance. It was trading in the 460s range. And we've been bullish on Micron just for some context here.

We've been bullish on Micron since it was in the high 60s range. And so we've been bullish on Micron. We've expected this kind of super cycle to happen. But what we were signaling on our last podcast, and we've been signaling to investors in our investing group, Tech Contrarians, is that everything has a shelf life and it's always important to remember that.

So everything has a shelf life and Micron and memory are no exception. So our concern for Micron was that for the quarter reported in December and for the print that we got two weeks ago, the upside that we're seeing the financial outperformance has been driven not by HBM, but instead by non-AI price uplift. By non-AI basically trading at higher prices because of this shortage.

So when the market woke up to the reality that AI is memory bound and that we're going to need a lot more memory than we have supply for at the moment, Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron reallocated manufacturing capacity from non-AI into HBM.

And so this caused a shortage on the non-AI front. And everyone knows where you have a shortage, you're going to have prices go up because the market, the supply is getting squeezed. And so we had prices surge for DRAM for NAND, we had prices surge across the board.

And for DRAM, it was specifically dramatic. We had prices surge in some for some kits over 300%. And what we saw with Micron's report is that non-HBM, specifically this past report, what it highlighted to confirm our thesis that this upside is coming from non-AI rather than AI is that non-HBM gross margins were actually higher than HBM gross margins.

And so we know that AI comes with higher ASP. So that doesn't make sense in essence, or at least it doesn't make sense for it to continue sustainably. That was the data point that we needed to really confirm our thesis that Micron's outperformance, the company is well positioned, their business is great, but the outperformance that's driving this insane stock rally is tied to the non-AI.

On that front, it's a cycle and a cycle does have a shelf life.

So we downgraded Micron post earnings two Tuesdays ago, and it's down something like 26 % since. And our thesis really there is that they're going to lose that leverage on non-AI higher ASP. And we're going to see DRM prices, spot prices, and contract prices really moderate into the year's end.

And part of what ties into this is that on the non-AI front, even heading into this AI rally, on the non-AI front, it's not like PC and smartphone, TAM units have been expanding substantially. So this isn't really a demand created shortage, it's a supply created shortage.

And there's a huge difference between those two. Because if you don't have the demand, then when memory prices go up and we get warnings from guys like Dell or Lenovo or Asus, we're gonna see demand contract because prices have gotten so high.

We even heard about higher memory costs from the big guys like Apple (AAPL). So demand is contracting. Smartphone and PC-TAM for the year from IDC have actually been revised down in terms of outlook. I think it was last September when they first issued their smartphone-TAM outlook for 2026, they were expecting 1.2 % growth. And then that outlook was revised down in December to a decline of 0.9% citing the memory shortage.

Then again, IDC cut numbers more dramatically, now expecting smartphone 10 to contract 12.9%. So by double digit percentage. And then the same thing is accurate on the PC front. Things aren't doing much better there as well. So the IDC's baseline outlook that was shared last November when it comes to PC was for a 2.4 % decline.

And now that's been revised all the way down to 11.3 % decline as of March, and some of the industry players are actually pointing to even a steeper decline of 15 % for the year.

So if we're seeing PC and smartphone contracts, and this is something that was confirmed by Micron's management on the earnings call two weeks ago, then that's all the more confirmation that this shortage is not going to drag out for too long. Because when a shortage happens, prices go up, everyone hoards memory because they want to get in at a good price.

But eventually, if the demand doesn't come to take out that inventory, then the inventory just stockpiles. And when inventory stockpiles, you're going to, as a buyer, you're going to stop buying that memory.

And so guys like Micron aren't going to benefit from that higher ASP on non-AI. And if I have a bunch of inventory that I'm going to sell it to whatever demand is left, rather than stockpiling more and more for a market that isn't really showing robust demand, if you see what I mean.

Rena Sherbill: When stocks move as much as they have in the past few days, few weeks, with the war and as you mentioned on headline driven news, how do you encourage subscribers and investors to take those movements - to strictly look at the long-term picture?

Tech Contrarians: So I think in a backdrop like this, and I'm going to circle back at the end of this just really quickly back to memory, just to address the turbo quant from Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), because I think it's played a big role in the sell off we've seen also since Micron earnings.

But on that note in specific, I think with the volatility that we're seeing, I think we're seeing a very reactionary market. And it really goes back into that dynamic that we discussed earlier, which is that everyone wants to make sure to be positioned at the bottom in time for that relief rally.

And this is really conditioning from last April and what we saw play out there. And so that's why we're seeing this kind of volatility. And from a fundamental standpoint, this kind of volatility trades divorced from the fundamentals.

When it comes to this, this is when we think it's best time to take advantage of the pullback and build positions or add to positions in the stocks that have very strong fundamental basis.

So for example, Marvell (MRVL) is a stock that we turned bullish on earlier this quarter. And that's doing exceptionally well today with a stake from Nvidia. On pullbacks like the ones we've been seeing over the past couple of weeks, that's the time to revisit the companies that are very well positioned, especially within tech that are not trading on their fundamentals because sooner or later the market will catch up to the fundamentals, especially once it's done from all the current distractions that are weighing it down.

And so I think that's the best cure to FOMO and it's the best treatment for the current market.

And then really quickly, I feel the need to address this because it's been such a big part of the memory conversation. And I think that a lot of the time people get carried away with what TurboQuant really means for the memory space, at least on the immediate front. we had Google's TurboQuant come out. It's not entirely new. In fact, they actually published an academic paper about this, I think it was last year.

And then this year they came out and they published a blog post about it. And that was really the marketing that it needed for it to get picked up by, you know, the market and by investors. And the impact that this blog post had is that essentially it crashed memory and storage tapes.

And we saw this across the board last week. It crashed SanDisk (SNDK), Micron, even storage guys like Seagate, Western Digital. And the reaction was somewhat dramatic, but it's not ill founded.

And essentially what the Google release implies is that there's a reduced use of memory for AI while carrying out LLM inferencing. So you need six times less memory, if we were to put it briefly. We get into the nitty-gritty of this from a fundamental perspective within the group. But just not to bore you with that now, the overall takeaway that the market got from this is that we actually don't need as much memory as we thought that we did.

And while that makes sense in a picture perfect world, the way that we're looking at this is not as revolutionary as much as it's as evolutionary. So we're seeing AI evolve. We say this on and off again. We're still in the very early cycles of early innings of AI. And the TurboQuant is just further indicator of that.

There's more efficient and productive ways that we can use memory. The CV cache is memory usage has been one of the biggest bottlenecks when it comes to LLM inferencing.

And so Google is basically saying that they perhaps on the problem, a solution to solve that problem. And that that could really bring down the cost of memory with the current shortage and really carry out much more productive AI models.

That is true, but I think this is a stepping point going forward, so it's still something that needs to be built upon. So it's not an overnight, I wouldn't say this is overnight why we are seeing DRAM spot prices come down. Instead, I think this is going to take longer to develop. And even once it's developed, then I don't think that it's going to reduce the overall need for memory.

Instead, I think it makes more sense to think about this as reallocating that memory supply that we now are building for to scale out AI at an even greater scale.

Rena Sherbill: I don't want to get outside of tech, but there has been an overlap between energy and AI for quite some time. And energy is in the headlines for more oil driven reasons these days. But what would you say about names slightly tangentially related or very tangentially related to the tech sector, but not quite in the tech sector?

Tech Contrarians: When it comes to kind of resolving the current bottleneck that we have on energy?

Rena Sherbill: Yes, and in general, how investors should think about the peripheral parts of the tech sector.

Tech Contrarians: I think energy is on everyone's mind now because of the massive requirements we're seeing on the AI front, but also because of the current situation playing out with Iran and the US.

And I would say, looking at energy through the lens of tech, through the lens of AI, I think the main thing that everyone should know is that the grid is really not built for the kind of energy requirements that we are looking at now or that we're trying to plan for going forward into 2027 and then into 2028.

So there really is, I think that would be the most painful bottleneck that we have in the realm of AI today we have huge AI factories being built out, but we still don't have guarantees on the energy that's gonna be able to service these. And so when it comes to that, I think that's where the market is still trying to find its stable footing.

I think there are north of 500 planned data center projects totaling north of 120 gigawatts of capacity on the global pipeline. And this is impacting retail electricity prices. This is impacting data centers own power consumption this year and next year. So if we take a holistic look of where AI and energy requirements are headed, I think that's a major gap in the market now that everyone is actively trying to address.

I think this is why we're seeing guys like Oklo (OKLO), where Sam Altman had been on the board a couple years ago really take off and be more center stage. But even names like that, which we actually downgraded, I think it was October of last year, even names like that are at higher risk because they're still not a tangible fundamental basis for the recycling of, for example, nuclear energy.

So I think there's a lot more questions than answers that we have on that front at the moment. And so the way we're looking at it is that it's going to be based on capacity needs and it's going to be for each individual tier one company.

I think that's how we're going to see it segmentized at the end of the day, especially after Trump's State of Union address and kind of how he addressed the situation when it comes to the big players like Meta (META), like Microsoft (MSFT), like Google, like Amazon (AMZN) needing to really take care of their own energy needs.

Because once again, the grid was not built for this kind of situation and the Iran war and what's happening there only makes this kind of fear or this concern on that front worse.

Rena Sherbill: Speaking to the geopolitical factors and the supply chain of tech and all its permutations and various details that are involved with that, you know, when we're talking geopolitically, obviously this past month, it's mostly based around the Middle East and parts of Europe, but there's also the China conversation as it pertains to tech.

There was the Super Micro (SMCI) conversation earlier this month. How are you thinking about, how are you continuing to think about, has anything changed about how you're thinking about that connection in tech?

Tech Contrarians: The Super Micro scandal was extremely interesting. I think it was the highest profile crackdown on AI chip smuggling that we've gotten to date. And this didn't happen too long ago with everything happening.

It feels like it's been a couple of weeks ago, but it was only on I think March 20th that everything really went down and we had the DOJ unseal an indictment charging the co-founder of Super Micro and others at the company for allegedly illegally basically selling billion dollars worth of advanced Nvidia chips to China.

And I think the immediate reaction or the immediate takeaway that we got from that is that there is a widening gap between the US and China that I think sometimes sell side research or the news tries to hush down as if China is really catching up with Huwei and the domestic solutions and the SN series as if they're very close to catching up to the US.

I know that's something that Jensen says a lot when talking about the US-China chip wars. And I think that China is executing well. They are working on achieving that self-sufficiency.

But what the smuggling scandal really comes to remind us is that the gap is very wide. And with Vera Rubin coming out in Q3, it'll only widen further. And so the fact that we're seeing so much smuggling means there is that demand domestically in China.

And the fact that Nvidia is still not in that market while AMD's chips, their MI308 have been sold in that market, think reaffirms that this whole entire situation is more geopolitical.

It has more to do with singling out Nvidia as the top AI GPU manufacturer rather than kind of saying that no to all US tech in general or saying that we have the technological capabilities that we wouldn't need to buy anything from the US because we're either seeing them buy from an AMD or we're seeing them be smuggled.

And so that's the first takeaway. And it really loops into what our expectation was earlier this year, which is that one way or another, Nvidia's advanced chips will make their way into China.

And that exists in a gray area, so we didn't want to make any accusations or really expand on that more. But our sense was that one way or another, these chips will make their way there because there is a widening performance gap. And China needs this to address their own domestic capabilities.

And so the situation with Super Micro only really reaffirmed that. And I think from here, we have two things that will happen simultaneously.

I think we'll see more harsh export restrictions from the US, from the DOJ specifically, after such a huge scandal was uncovered and it was happening at such a big scale.

And at the same time, I think that we'll see movement on the conversation between Trump and China. And wrapping this into the broader geopolitical tensions happening at the moment, part of why I think that we should see a resolution to situation in the Middle East sooner rather than later is because Trump has rebooked and scheduled his visit to China several times.

And it was kind of implied at the beginning of the conflict that he would want to be visiting China once the war had been resolved. And so the fact that we've seen him stick to the date after pushing the visit stick to the date of I think it's mid May the 14th to 16th around those dates kind of reaffirms that we should see a resolution before then.

And I think once we get that meeting, I'm also expecting that we're gonna get updates on the chip war front and the situation there, especially that the Iran war has brought up the conversation of will China invade Taiwan, which has been this on and off going conversation really around TSMC as the golden shield for the island. And so with that, I think we could see NVIDIA move into the region once again.

But I think that was something that's outside of Johnson's control very visibly now. And I think it has a lot more to do with the conversation between Trump and Xi that'll happen in May. And especially with the Vera Rubin coming in the second half of the year, once again, the specs on Vera Rubin I think will only very dramatically widen that performance gap.

So perhaps we see a new package offered from the US towards China where it comes to selling less advanced chips to get the ball moving there since there's already smuggling happening.

And I think Trump is a businessman first and foremost. if there's any parts being sold, I think that the US definitely wants that 25 % fee that they make off of it.

Rena Sherbill: What else would you add to this conversation?

Tech Contrarians: So I would say when it comes to tech, think everyone is a bit spooked. Once again, heading into this, markets were a bit spooked from tech. The whole entire Mag-7 are in the negative.

As of last I checked this morning, they're all in the negatives. They probably need to reconsider a name change for 2026, considering how much these guys have gotten hammered down from Meta to Microsoft, especially those two have come down quite a bit.

But what I would say taking a step back and looking at tech in general is that the market isn't as convinced by the narrative, or I would say the easy money is gone. The easy money to make from tech, the easy money is off the table at the moment. so now this is when, although the market isn't looking at it like that, this is when the fundamentals really come to shine through.

Because the guys that will be standing in a year from now in the green and really doing well are the guys whose components are needed in this infrastructure build out, or the guys who the industry transition will push them as their catalyst.

So something that's not necessarily attached to end customer demand, but more attached to the planning and the buildup that's happening at the moment. I think that's where investors specifically in tech should look to position themselves.

And when it comes to fundamentals, when you're owning these kinds of names, you're more comfortable on red days because the panic isn't as bad because you're thinking, all right, but based on the technicals within the tech industry based on how the industry roadmap is going, these guys will come back because their components are needed or their power management content, for example, like monolithic power is going to grow.

So for those guys, I think that's the appeal.

And then the second thing I was going to say is that given the war situation, in the market, there's always more opportunity, right? If you take a different angle, there's always more opportunity. So it's really important not to look at this market and try to find the same things that we were able to the same trends that we were able to ride easily in 2025.

Again, the easy money is gone. From here, I would say you got to change your perspective and be adaptable and versatile to the current market. And if we look at it from that angle, especially for shorter term investors, that's where I see appeal in the analog peer group.

We have a working thesis on that front, which is that when we had the situation last April with the liberation day and the 90 day pause, analog guys outperformed the following quarter because there was a lot of double ordering, there was a lot of pull-in because people are worried that the supply disruption was going to only get worse.

So they wanted to get ahead of the game. And so if we see a similar thing kind of happen in reaction to the oil disruption that we've got over the past month, then we could see these guys be somewhat of a safe haven for the short term in the current environment.

Although, from an end market perspective, they're still struggling, but this could be a way to kind of take another angle, look at the tech space from there and think, all right, where is the double ordering going to happen?

Where could there be potential upside on panic buying ahead of what's feared to be potentially a longer disrupt supply shock or the supply disruption?

Rena Sherbill: Sara, do you have a motto that you live by? Like personally or professionally?

Tech Contrarians: I mean, wow, you put me on the spot with that.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah, I asked somebody that question last week and I started thinking that I'm going to start asking people that I feel like might have a good answer to it.

Tech Contrarians: It's a great question. A motto that I live by is... let me take a minute to really think about this to get you the right response. It depends on how you interpret it, but I do tell myself this a lot, that the market isn't going anywhere in the sense that for me on a personal level, since you asked what's my motto personally, the market isn't going anywhere in the sense that we've seen so many dramatic crashes in this market.

And of course, you don't have any two dramatic downturns that are carbon copies of one another, but the market's been through so much. And at the end of the day, there's always something else that's going to happen. It's always forward looking. Whatever is in the past is something to learn from. And then whatever you have going forward is going to have all the more experience to gain out of it.

So the market's not going anywhere in the sense that at the end of the day, you can look at your screen all day. You can take a break and do something else. But at end of the day, the market's still going to be there. And I think that's the beauty of the market.

It's organized chaos. And to me, that's the most fascinating thing.

No matter what happens, the market's always going to come back. There's going to be stocks that do well. There's going to be stocks that don't do well. And there's opportunity in it all.

So I think that that's what keeps me going on a daily basis, that this is like a game. It's an equation to be solved. And the fundamentals make it all the more fun because you have these cool tools to use along the way.

Rena Sherbill: Put the fun in fundamentals.

Tech Contrarians: I should use that one next time around.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah. Put it on a t-shirt. Get some merch up at Tech Contrarians. Sara, I really appreciate this conversation. I always appreciate you coming on, investing experts. Again, your investing group is Tech Contrarians on Seeking Alpha. Happy for you to share anything else with listeners or if you would like to have the last word, it's yours.

Tech Contrarians: I just want to let everyone know, feel free to reach us at Tech Contrarians, shoot us a DM. We have one-on-one consultation calls behind our paywall, which has been the most popular feature so far, I'm glad to say. So feel free to sign up, test out that feature, test out Tech Contrarians, and let us know your thoughts.
