data.title: Bicara: Maintaining 'Buy' Rating On Ficerafusp Alfa Phase 3 Advancement
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Bicara Therapeutics Inc. (BCAX), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Bicara: Stock Decline On FICERA Abstract Release Creates Buying Opportunity." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company had released its abstract presentation for the development of its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] for the treatment of patients with 1st-line recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC].

What I went over last time was only that of the objective response rate [ORR] releases as part of the abstract for the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting. What I'm going to provide an update for today is the release of additional data from the phase 1/1b trial using ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat these patients with 1st-line R/M HNSCC.

In addition, the company has made significant progress since then, because in Q4 of 2025 it initiated its phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study using this ficerafusp alfa combination to treat these specific 1st-line HPV-negative R/M HNSCC patients. Why should investors care about this program? That's because the company believes that it can provide an interim analysis from this pivotal phase 3 study in mid-2027.

Speaking of this study, what I will also state below is that the company not only selected 1500 mg of ficerafusp alfa as the optimal dose to move forward with for this study, but clinical testing allowed it to move to a more optimal loading dose and once every 3 weeks [Q3W] maintenance dosing schedule for KEYTRUDA. Today, I'm going to maintain a Buy rating for this stock based on the progress it has made to date. Bicara traded lower on the back of Merus' results with EGFRxLGR5 bispecific antibody petosemtamab, but the thing with this is that this drug is focused on both 1st-line and/or 2nd-line/3rd-line PD-L1-positive recurrent/metastatic [R/M] HNSCC patients. Whereas the focus for Bicara is specifically targeting 1st-line R/M HNSCC who are HPV negative [HPV-].

A positive is that Genmab A/S (GMAB) acquired Merus N.V. to get its hands on petosemtamab. Still, I place my Buy rating for the time being with some inherent risk in terms of Genmab's drug targeting these HNSCC patients. While Bicara showed a 12-month overall survival [OS] rate of 61% for its targeted HPV-negative patient population, Merus' drug achieved a 79% OS rate. It remains to be seen what happens, but with Genmab expected to potentially release data this year, this is a risk to keep in mind for Bicara. Lastly, the company is expected to share follow-up data from its phase 1/1b study of ficerafusp alfa as well.

With the initiation of the phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study using ficerafusp alfa to treat patients with 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC, plus several catalysts expected over the next few years, I believe that Bicara Therapeutics should remain with a Buy rating.

Additional Data Given And On The Way To Move Ficerafusp Alfa Program Forward

As I noted above, the company was able to initiate its phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study using its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta drug ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 1st-line recurrent and/or metastatic HPV-negative HNSCC. Before going over this clinical program in detail, since when I last spoke about it, plus any catalysts to come about that investors should keep an eye on, I believe it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma [HNSCC] is a type of cancer that arises on the mucosal surfaces of the mouth, nose, throat, and/or larynx of a patient. The type of cancer a patient gets is dependent upon its location. For instance, if it occurs in the mouth of a patient, the cause could be due to tobacco or alcohol use. On the other hand, pharynx and/or larynx could be caused by the human papillomavirus [HPV], primarily HPV-16. I bring this up because HNSCC is broken down into two different types, and they are as follows:

  • HPV-positive patients.
  • HPV-negative patients.

The recurrent global Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma market is expected to reach $4.61 billion by 2030. This is a large market opportunity, but the key emphasis for Bicara is to use its drug ficerafusp alfa to only go after these first-line patients with HPV-negative disease. In terms of market opportunity, specifically going after these types of patients, it exists. It is believed that between 80% and 90% of HNSCC patients are in the R/M setting [Bicara Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #9]. An additional item to note is the huge unmet medical need in place, as these HPV-negative patients are worse off than those of HPV positive patients. Median overall survival [mOS] of HPV-negative HNSCC patients that take the current standard of care [SOC] doesn't fare very well:

  • KEYTRUDA alone mOS of 9 months.
  • KEYTRUDA plus chemotherapy mOS of 7 months.

The mechanism of action [MOA] of ficerafusp alfa is to use the EGFR component to target the tumor, of course, but there remains a key issue with being able to adequately penetrate the tumor microenvironment [TME], and this would be TGF-beta signaling. This signaling pathway is what allows the tumor to create resistance and protect itself. Thus, the TGF-beta is attached to EGFR through a domain to form the drug and create a long-lasting, durable response.

The company has already been able to initiate its phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study using its drug ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC. It is important to note that the company is making good progress with this study because, as I will be showing below, it has made significant progress since I last wrote about it. First and foremost, what I revealed the last time around with the abstract release for ASCO 2025 was that the company showed its drug combination to achieve a 64% objective response rate [ORR], or 18 out of 28 patients. The new data that was presented at ASCO 2025 revealed additional data which, truthfully, gave the company the "green light" to continue to explore the use of this ficerafusp alfa combination to treat this specific group of HNSCC patients. It was noted that there was a two-year survival [OS] rate of 46% [median OS 21.3 months]. In a setting where these patients have an unmet medical need, this is pretty good.

In addition, in prior data before ASCO 2025, it was noted that the 12-month OS rate for HPV-negative 1st-line HNSCC patients was 61%. I'm bringing this up for two reasons. One reason is because Merus had revealed data from its study showing a 12-month OS of 79% with petosemtamab. Despite Merus having reported this data, plus causing Bicara's stock to trade lower, it shouldn't be a big deal. I think one reason is because you can't really rely on cross-trial comparisons for starters. Secondly, because ficerafusp alfa is being developed to have a focus on targeting 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients. On the other hand, Merus is targeting a broader patient population of 1st-line PD-L1-positive R/M HNSCC patients.

This remains a risk for Bicara, of course, but the data it obtained from its phase 1/1b study is good enough to continue exploring into phase 3 clinical testing. Remember above, where I stated that the MOA is to overcome resistance of TGF-beta signaling and drive deeper tumor penetration? Well, this next set of data proves this factor. It was noted that out of 28 HPV-negative R/M HNSCC patients who took the ficerafusp alfa plus KEYTRUDA combination ended up achieving a confirmed ORR of 54% [15 out of 28 patients]. Of these 15 who responded to this combination therapy, they had a median duration of response [mDOR] of 21.7 months [Bicara Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #11]. The point here is that the drug was able to penetrate tumors efficiently because of the MOA I just mentioned above.

Chemotherapy + an EGFR alone is not enough to drive deep types of responses. The company found that its combination does extremely well in the targeting of HPV-negative HNSCC patients. The end conclusion is that this drug is indeed able to remodel the TME and drive deeper responses. Such deep responses are important in being able to help boost PFS and OS rates as well. This is noted with the fact that 12 out of 15 patients [80%] achieved such a response, showing ≥80% tumor shrinkage.

With respect to the dose that was selected for the phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study, it is the following:

  • Ficerafusp alfa 1500 mg once weekly [QW].
  • KEYTRUDA Q3W.

Such a loading dose and then a Q3W maintenance dose were established to offer convenience for the patient but at the same time maintain safety/efficacy as well. The point is that the testing it did allowed it to explore less frequent dosing to improve convenience [Ficerafusp Alfa Corporate Presentation testing 2000 mg Q2W, slide #10]. The company believes that it will have its phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 pivotal study almost fully enrolled by the end of this year. However, a major catalyst for this entire program would be the release of results from it.

It is expected that interim data from this phase 3 study using ficerafusp alfa to treat these 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients will be released in mid-2027. That's a long time away, but there is another catalyst to consider, which is that long-term follow-up data from the phase 1b study using this drug combination to treat these same HNSCC patients will be presented at the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology [ASCO] Annual Meeting, to be held from May 29 to June 2, 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Bicara Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $414.8 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company was able to raise a significant amount of cash with the closing of an oversubscribed public offering and the full exercise of underwriters' option to purchase additional shares. The total gross proceeds raised from this offering of $161.8 million were achieved on February 26, 2026. I believe that this company should have cash for an extended period of time. Why is that? The reason why is because the company has given guidance that it believes the cash it had beforehand, plus the $161.8 million raised from the oversubscribed offering, will be enough to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2029.

The company burns about $41.1 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $33 million in R&D expenses and then $8.1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Bicara Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be the ongoing development of its lead bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA for the treatment of patients with 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC in the ongoing pivotal phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 study.

The risk here is that even though the company was able to achieve a 12-month OS rate of 61% in its phase 1/1b study using this ficerafusp alfa combination to treat these patients, there is no assurance that it will be replicated again in the ongoing phase 3 pivotal study. Not only that, but any data that Genmab releases for petosemtamab treating 1st-line/2nd-line/3rd-line PD-L1-positive R/M HNSCC patients will be compared to that of what Bicara has released thus far. Thus, it's possible that a win for Genmab in its late-stage testing could mean a lower stock price for Bicara.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity that the company is working on. It wants to test its theory of a "pipeline in a pill." To this end, it expects to present data from its phase 1b expansion cohort evaluating the use of ficerafusp alfa both as a monotherapy and in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 3rd-line metastatic colorectal cancer [mCRC] who are wild-type microsatellite stable [MSS] and have RAS/BRAF mutations. Such data is expected to be released in the 2nd half of 2026.

The risk here is that even though this drug has been shown to work well with KEYTRUDA in the targeting of 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients, there is no assurance that it will work in 3rd-line mCRC patients. Should data from this trial be disappointing, then the only value driver for this company going forward will be that of its lead program of targeting HPV-negative HNSCC patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be the expected release of long-term follow-up data from the phase 1b study using ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA for the treatment of 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients at the ASCO 2026 Annual Meeting. The risk here is that even though the data shown thus far has been positive, there is no guarantee that the next set of data to be released is going to cause the stock price to trade higher or boost shareholder value. In addition, it's quite possible that if the long-term follow-up data doesn't meet investors' expectations, then this could also cause the stock price to trade lower as well.

Conclusion

Bicara Therapeutics has done well to get to the point of where it is. That's because it has been able to achieve positive results from its phase 1/1b study using ficerafusp alfa plus KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC. As I have shown above, it has been able to do extremely well zeroing in on the specific focus of going after HPV-negative patients. With this subpopulation, it has shown its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta ficerafusp alfa in combination with KEYTRUDA to achieve a 12-month OS of 61%. I have set out a Buy rating on this stock based on what it has attained thus far from its phase 1/1b study targeting these patients.

Not only that, but it has already initiated the pivotal phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 study as well. The importance of this is the expectation of having most of the patients for this study enrolled by the end of this year and then releasing an interim analysis from this in mid-2027. In terms of my Buy rating, I'm keeping it for now because it is not always prudent to follow cross-trial comparisons. Thus, even with Merus' having a slightly better 12-month OS rate, it doesn't mean that this company is still not making significant progress.

With the initiation of the phase 3 FORTIFI-HN01 study of ficerafusp alfa plus KEYTRUDA targeting 1st-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients, plus several catalysts over the next few years, I believe it is important to maintain a Buy rating. The buyout of Merus by Genmab might even be a bullish aspect to consider in terms of Bicara's program, in my opinion.

data.title: 5 Overlooked AI Stocks For The Market Rebound
data.description:
Introduction

Just a couple of days ago, I wrote an article covering my top 6 picks for a market rebound. In that article, I explained which stocks make up the “core” of my risky investment portfolio, and, of course, those were the large or fast-growing players, among them Micron (MU), Uber (UBER), and a couple of Mag 7 stocks.

This is a risky portfolio because it is mostly made up of tech names, with some additions from healthcare, retail, etc. In this portfolio, I don’t have indices, staples, bonds, dividends, or anything like that - for this purpose, I have a separate fund and a pension account, both of which I am not managing actively. But of course, in my risky portfolio, besides the “core” holdings (which are mostly known and well-established companies), I add a mix of different, smaller, riskier plays, which could potentially make better returns.

I know that under current conditions, with the war, the oil supply constraints, and overall uncertainty, it doesn’t feel natural to start “picking up” overlooked AI stocks, but it might end in huge returns if we do exactly that. Many of the stocks I considered or even added in the second half of 2025 trade for a major discount from their highs, so today, I’ll share some of the most interesting AI stocks, which deserve to be in your watchlist and potentially in your portfolio.

These are NOT the only AI stocks I follow and consider buying, and there is a couple of reasons why I chose these specific names for this article: they delivered good results lately, they have real clients, revenue/margin expansion and other metrics by which we can be sure it’s not a “bubble,” they are down significantly from highs, they are little discussed just because the momentum isn’t there, and as a result, they could potentially become the best overlooked AI plays for the market rebound, whenever it happens. It doesn’t mean these stocks have a limited downside. They all can go even lower depending on the business and overall market conditions, but I tried choosing stocks that, in my opinion, have a way more meaningful upside than downside.

Symbotic Inc. (SYM)

The first name is Symbotic, and this is a major player in AI-powered robotics for warehouse automation. I chose this stock as a first name because I was seriously considering buying Symbotic in November 2025, but after comparing the risk to the valuation, I decided to wait. I wrote an article about this company then, and even in the title, I said that I’m not ready to take this risk, so I rated it a Hold. But a lot had changed since that piece, and today I’ll share what exactly and why the risk-reward ratio looks much more compelling right now. Before we jump right into it, for those who don’t know what Symbotic is, here is a quote from my previous breakdown:

Symbotic is a warehouse automation company that builds full automation systems for large enterprises. They take a regular warehouse and transform it into a much more optimized version of itself, where most of the work is done by their technology (robots powered by AI software). They design and manufacture this hardware themselves, which includes the robots. The company also does the software and the AI parts. Their biggest customer is Walmart, which most people already know, and they also work with companies like Albertsons.

Straight after this quote, we can see that the company is progressing. While Walmart remains its largest customer, and in general, it would be difficult to find another client of this size (given that Amazon (AMZN) does its own automation), Symbotic managed to bring on another impressive name. As of 2026, their new customer is Medline, which is a huge medical-surgical distributor with over 29 million sq ft of warehouse space across 69 global distribution centers. This is Symbotic’s first customer in the health vertical, but among the distributors, it’s also one of the largest ones. This is great news, as it addresses my concern over customer concentration. It’s not solving it, of course, as Walmart still makes about 80% of Symbotic's revenues, but also, this partnership has progressed to the deployment stage, which is less risky than the preparations.

As I am writing this, it looks like another partnership with a grocery player came in for an Automation Project at the Gulf Coast Division Support Center, which shows that the technology is valuable and the company is expanding.

Symbotic has a massive $22.3 billion backlog, but it is not expected to come in this year or even next year, so I’d take this number with a grain of salt. On this stage, customers can still pull back if something changes, and by the way, the recession, or a fear of it, is a great “something” to pull out of unnecessary spending. Also, when companies have a large backlog, it’s relatively easy to impress the market on the top line, exactly what happened last quarter with Symbotic, but it’s not always easy to keep the bottom line intact. In the latest quarter, the company managed to beat across the board, and this is one of the reasons I am ready to seriously consider this stock for my portfolio.

Another reason is Cash From Operations, which is usually volatile because the company doesn’t recognize the revenue right away. I explained this mechanism in the last article. But what is important is that the trend is clear, and the company is improving (unfortunately, I wasn’t able to make a trendline, but the direction is visible enough without it).

The best part is the valuation - in my last article, I looked at the price-to-operating cash flow, and it had been at 26 times. Today, it went down to 6 times, thanks to the growth in CFO, but also a meaningful price correction of 17% YTD.

The biggest worry of the shareholders before had been not the concentration, but the ability of Symbotic to deploy those systems on time. In Q1 2026, Symbotic had 51 operational systems, up from 29 in the same quarter in 2025. They have 57 more systems in deployment, and while the company is still not incredibly efficient, it takes them now about 10 months to finish a project. Once again, it’s not perfect, but it’s a major improvement from about 24 months we saw back in 2024.

UiPath, Inc. (PATH)

UiPath is one of the companies I personally really like, and in order not to “miss” it, I opened a very small position (under 1% of my risky portfolio) at the relatively expensive level (it was already in 2026, I bought at about $14.5), to increase this position when the stock is going to be cheaper. This is something I do for stocks, which I’d really want to add, and I think they are too important just to sit on the “watchlist.”

This is a very interesting play, because it is a pure AI investment, and this is still rare. UiPath, in general, is an automation platform. It means it can go over large amounts of data and look for patterns, which could help a business to automate that process. It can physically go and perform some repetitive tasks instead of human workers in order to make it about 10-times faster. This is called RPA or robotic process automation, which, if simplified, works as "if-this-then-that." But as of 2026, this is not the main story anymore, because this company has added Agentic AI (can both reason and, most importantly, act), which is believed to be the future. The whole story around the various types of “claws” and all of the frenzy around OpenClaw is the same thing, just that it is a dangerous open model, which, in my opinion, should be restricted to use, and UiPath is a controlled Agentic AI for enterprises. If you are not sure what I am referring to, I suggest reading my article about OpenClaw to understand this whole story better.

Even though I am very skeptical and even negative about OpenClaw, the popularity of it right now actually helps UiPath, as before, investors thought AI could kill UiPath, but the rise of tools like OpenClaw shows that LLMs can make automation easier to build. And it’s not one or the other, and that Agentic AI is actually an advanced part of the technology.

UiPath also had a great quarter, closing its 2026 fiscal year with a strong performance going into the new AI agents era. The company beat expectations with quarterly revenue reaching $481 million and achieved a major milestone by posting its first-ever year of full GAAP profitability. Management highlighted that the business is at an inflection point where their new agentic AI products are becoming the main thing in the enterprise deals, with a significant majority of their million-dollar customers already adopting these tools. By cutting down the time it takes to build and deploy complex workflows from weeks to hours, UiPath is effectively clearing the automation backlog for its clients.

Guidance for 2027 was the best point of the update. Management expects to reach the $2 billion in annual recurring revenue, and it looks like they want to focus on high-stakes industries like healthcare and finance, which is very important to me, because these are the industries with no room for error. The ability of UiPath to enter and operate them would be the best indication of its security and correct execution.

The stock is majorly undervalued, reflecting AI disruption fears over software stocks, but I think those fears are irrational, so I am buying software myself and view this as a great opportunity. I’ll add to my PATH position at this level.

SentinelOne, Inc. (S)

I might surprise you with SentinelOne, as this is a cybersecurity player, but I think it fits well with the character of an investor who would be interested in this article. If you’re reading this and considering the stocks I discuss, it means you are bullish on AI as an industry, and you’re ready for some volatility. Sentinel is a perfect choice here!

SentinelOne uses AI to autonomously treat threats, and the company calls itself "AI Security Leader." You see, there is a massive shift happening in the cyber world. Legacy solutions are being replaced by "AI-Native" platforms, which are all the buzz now, and SentinelOne is most likely the main player in this niche.

Now, it doesn’t mean the “niche” will prove to be winning, not necessarily, but as long as there are customers and demand, Sentinel is perfectly positioned to capture it. Most cybersecurity companies added AI as a "feature" not so long ago, just to participate and to be a part of the AI rally, while SentinelOne’s platform (Singularity) was built to be autonomous from the very beginning. They have, for example, an AI that can reconstruct the attack when it happens, and it can automatically restore a computer to its pre-infected state. Besides, they have Purple AI, which is a generative AI security assistant, and according to the latest data, it was included in over 50% of all new deals. They also recently launched Prompt AI Agent Security at the RSAC 2026 conference, and it governs other AI agents (like the OpenClaw ones we discussed).

This is incredible, because just as I said, OpenClaw is a major threat; here we already have a partial solution, which is also AI. So effectively, AI will be watching over AI. Once again, as long as everyone talks about it, Sentinel wins, so be it. Maybe they’ll train from early deployment and will use that knowledge later for a more complex solution.

In general, the discussion about cyber, while absolutely fascinating, isn’t my favorite due to its difficulty. I have a pretty extensive coverage on the matter here, on Seeking Alpha, and when it comes to breaking down stocks in this industry, it always requires hours of research. I suggest reading an article about why Anthropic (ANTHRO) will not be able to disrupt cyber companies, and why they are majorly oversold due to AI fears. There, I explain how many different types of threats and problems companies can encounter and what solutions are required to solve those.

Sentinel is not different, and it is probably the cheapest cybersecurity player out there. It was cheaper than its larger peers for a long time because it was struggling to get to profitability. On the recent earnings, they highlighted a “$1 billion revenue scale, growing 22% year-over-year and delivered full year operating profitability, a significant milestone towards profitable growth,” but it didn’t seem to help much. The stock still trades at a discount to its peers - it currently trades at 3.5 forward sales, which is not straightforwardly cheap, but considering it’s leading position in AI adoption, it deserves to trade at a slight premium to the sector.

Unity Software Inc. (U)

To make this article more interesting and “picks” more diversified, I want to mention Unity Software, which is in the gaming industry, but it is increasingly integrating AI into all of its business operations. Here’s what they do on paper:

Unity Software Inc. operates a platform to develop, deploy, and grow games and interactive experiences for mobile phones, PCs, consoles, and extended reality devices.

This company has some incredible results and adoption already, as over 70% of all mobile games are powered by it, and a huge portion of augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) experiences are made with Unity’s help. Unity has a “creative” side of the business with specialized workshops where developers build digital worlds, but they also have the “ad” part, which is even more successful. Unity runs a massive advertising and monetization network that helps developers find players and show them relevant ads.

The AI part is especially interesting for the ad segment, as the company uses deep learning to analyze billions of data points from the Unity Runtime, which is the software that executes games, and millions of users have it installed. It can predict which player is most likely to buy an in-game item with incredible accuracy, and it already transforms into tangible results. This segment grew 48% year-over-year in Q1 2026. Besides, the company just launched some kind of an AI assistant which will be helping in development, and not only in advertising.

Unity delivered an impressive quarter, and my colleague Ian Bezek did a great job breaking it down just two days ago, so I suggest reading this article for the freshest results:

Unity generated a roughly 48% rise in its strategic Grow (advertising) revenues and a 14% rise in its Create (game engine) revenues for Q1 versus the prior year. Overall, that equates to a 34% y-o-y increase in strategic revenues. There's been a lot of concern in the analyst community over whether Unity could get back to growth after the downturn in the gaming industry coming out of the pandemic. I think, especially with the Q1 results, it's becoming increasingly difficult to deny Unity's operational momentum in both the game engine and the ads business.

SoundHound AI, Inc. (SOUN)

I traded SoundHound AI over a year ago, and it was an amazing trade. I bought it for around $4 per share and sold it for $15 to $17. I’ve always liked the company because their product is so easy to understand: they provide voice AI solutions for industries like automotive and restaurants, and they’re expanding into healthcare, retail, and financial services. They basically build voice-enabled AI agents for customer service and some other tasks. In cars, for example, they provide both the voice and the reasoning for the driver’s assistance, and their client list is already impressive, with names like BNP Paribas, Chipotle, GoDaddy, Kia, and Hyundai.

They have revenue and are currently showing about 60% quarter-over-quarter growth. While that’s slower than last year, it’s still a very high level to be at. SoundHound was on top of the world when Nvidia (NVDA) invested in it in 2025, but since Nvidia closed its position, the market seems to have forgotten they exist. The momentum is largely negative now, and the stock is down more than 30% year-to-date.

But be careful here, because even with this drop, the stock is still not cheap. I’ve been waiting for a lower entry point, though there’s always a chance I’ll miss it. For example, today, with the Nasdaq up 3%, SoundHound surged over 12%. It always plunges strongly due to high valuation multiples when the market is down, but it flies when the market is positive. If we enter another high-growth AI cycle, this stock has a massive runway and huge upside, though the downside risk remains significant, too.

I debated including SoundHound in this article because I view it more as a watchlist play or a momentum trade, which is why I traded it before. With expensive stocks like this, the best strategy is often to trade the momentum, but at the same time, this is not an "empty promise" AI company. They have real clients and real growth. My last trade doubled in a few months, which was great, but it was a small 2% position, and I wouldn’t make it larger.

data.title: Invesco Balanced-Risk Allocation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund underperformed its benchmark

data.title: Northrim BanCorp: Attractive Valuation Amid Geopolitical Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

Energy price volatility and the ongoing war in the Middle East have clouded the economic outlook around the world, pushing bond yields higher and equity prices lower. Banks are naturally quite sensitive to these developments, with shares of many financial institutions declining in the double digits on a year-to-date basis.

Crises, however, also create opportunities. Buyers in Asia will undoubtedly want to diversify their energy supplies. One beneficiary of this trend is Alaska, with the state's economy heavily dependent on the oil & gas industry. Investors interested in increasing their exposure to "The Last Frontier" state may want to consider Northrim BanCorp (NRIM). The shares are only modestly higher since I last covered the company back in July 2025, presenting an attractive entry point for investors. The bullish NRIM thesis can be summarized as:

  • NRIM trades at only 8.85x its 2026 expected earnings, presenting a ~14% discount relative to regional bank peers.
  • While reported earnings are forecast to decline this year, growth is likely to resume in 2027, as the 2026 drop is driven by the non-recurrence of positive one-offs that boosted 2025 performance.
  • Credit costs should remain contained as Alaska's economy benefits from its commodity exposure, with odds of a recession arguably smaller compared to other U.S. states.

For readers not familiar with NRIM, the bank's Q4 2025 results presentation provides a comprehensive overview of the company. In brief, NRIM operates exclusively in Alaska, with the $2.3 billion loan portfolio primarily focused on residential and commercial mortgages, as well as construction loans. These three categories account for roughly 75% of loans outstanding. The remaining 25% are allocated principally to commercial loans.

2025 Results Overview

NRIM reported EPS of $2.87/share in 2025, a stellar 73% Y/Y increase relative to 2024. A major contributor to the improved performance was higher other operating income, which benefited from one-off effects such as the sale of Pacific Wealth Advisors [PWA]. That said, underlying performance was quite strong, with net interest income increasing thanks to loan volume growth as well as an improvement in the net interest margin to 4.69% (2024: 4.28%).

Tangible book value stood at $12.47/share at the end of 2025, a 27.4% increase from 2024, as NRIM only paid $0.64/share in dividends last year while at the same time making no share buybacks. The bank remains well-capitalized with a CET1 ratio of 10.38%, well above the 8% regulatory requirement.

All in all, I think there were no major red flags with regard to 2025 results. Despite improving its net interest margin, NRIM managed to grow deposits by 10.5% Y/Y, only marginally below the 15.4% Y/Y increase in loans. As such, the loan-to-deposit ratio remained quite low at 0.79x. As a result, I believe NRIM has the flexibility to remain aggressive on deposit pricing, allowing it to maintain an attractive net interest margin.

2026 Outlook

While NRIM does not issue specific annual guidance, analysts expect the bank to deliver EPS of $2.60/share in 2026, a circa 9.4% decline relative to 2025. I believe the weaker performance is entirely due to the non-recurrence of positive one-off factors such as the sale of PWA.

Indeed, looking at Fed funds futures, the Fed is projected to remain on hold this year, indicating little pressure on the net interest margin from monetary policy changes. Likewise, loan growth remained robust into year-end 2025, with total loans increasing 3.4% Q/Q in Q4 2025 alone.

As such, barring a sharp slowdown in Alaska's economy, the dip in 2026 EPS should prove temporary. Case in point, analysts expect EPS to return to growth in 2027, reaching roughly $2.78/share next year.

To sum up, NRIM should see solid 2026 performance on an underlying basis, with reported results impacted by the non-recurrence of positive one-off factors that boosted 2025 results.

Valuation and Rating

While markets have been quite volatile in recent weeks, as I write this, NRIM trades at about $23.00/share, reflecting a circa 8x trailing P/E multiple. This remains notably cheaper than U.S. regional banks, which currently trade at about 12x their trailing earnings.

Of course, as discussed in the previous 2026 Outlook section, NRIM's EPS is projected to decline in 2026. As such, looking at forward P/E multiples arguably gives a fairer view as to the bank's valuation relative to peers. Taking the $2.60/share analyst EPS estimate, we see that NRIM trades at 8.85x its 2026 earnings, which still represents a notable ~14% discount to regional bank peers, which trade at about 10.25x their forward earnings.

In light of recent geopolitical events, I view this discount as excessive. If anything, the recession risk for Alaska's economy is below average over the near term thanks to the state's high exposure to rising commodity prices. In contrast, Goldman Sachs (GS) recently hiked its U.S. recession probability to 30%, citing the fallout from the Middle East war.

To sum up, notwithstanding risks, which I will discuss in the next section, I believe NRIM presents an attractive investment opportunity thanks to its robust operating performance and exclusive focus on Alaska, which I see as one of the beneficiaries of the need to diversify energy supplies away from the Middle East. NRIM's appeal is further amplified by the discount the bank trades at relative to regional banking peers. As such, I confirm my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Risks

As highlighted in the Introduction section, NRIM's main exposure is to the real estate sector, which accounts for roughly 75% of the loan portfolio. While nonperforming assets are currently quite low at 0.35% (marginally down relative to 2024), a wider deterioration of Alaska's economy could impact NRIM through higher net charge-offs.

This may principally come if we see a reversal of recent tailwinds such as high commodity prices and increased government spending on defense. Indeed, while high energy prices are a clear benefit in the near term, they also incentivize buyers in Asia to switch to renewables. As such, while economics for projects such as Alaska LNG look bulletproof in the next few years, over the medium to long term, Alaska's economy may suffer if we see a reversal in energy price dynamics.

The silver lining is that these risks are arguably reflected in NRIM stock, as it trades at a discount to regional bank peers, perhaps reflecting the more cyclical nature of Alaska's economy.

Credit risk aside, the other principal risk to consider is that NRIM has to maintain a competitive deposit offering, which will allow the bank to achieve an attractive net interest margin while pursuing loan growth opportunities. Here the bank boasts that it has increased its deposit market share to 17.5% in 2025, up 1.8% Y/Y. As such, NRIM's competitive position appears robust in the near term.

Conclusion

NRIM stock has posted double-digit declines so far in 2026, impacted by a broad risk-off move in equity markets. This comes even as the bank reported solid 2025 results and is projected to achieve strong underlying performance in 2026, notwithstanding the non-recurrence of positive one-offs which boosted 2025 EPS.

Against the backdrop of persistent geopolitical tensions and energy price volatility, Alaska is likely to emerge as a beneficiary of Asian buyers' demands for energy supply diversification. With an exclusive focus on Alaska, NRIM is one of the key beneficiaries of an expected acceleration in the state's economy. The bull case appeal is further amplified by the discount NRIM trades at relative to regional bank peers.

Key risks in the investment case include a potential increase in non-performing loans, a reversal of recent commodity price gains, and the need to maintain a competitive deposit offering in order to maintain robust net interest margins.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Positioning For Elevated Hormuz Risk: Fertilizer And Agriculture As Second-Order Opportunities
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Supply Chain Alpha as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Summary

The Strait of Hormuz handles ~20% of global oil flows and significant LNG exports. Even partial disruption can reprice energy and fertilizer markets. Historical precedent (2022) shows fertilizer equities can outperform during energy shocks. I see a favorable risk/reward in agriculture and fertilizer positioning via Invesco DB Agriculture Fund ETF (DBA), Teucrium Soybean Fund ETF (SOYB) (the October calls), and fertilizer equities: CF Industries Holdings (CF), Intrepid Potash (IPI), and Nutrien (NTR).

Introduction

Every now and then the market reminds you that geography still matters. The Strait of Hormuz is one of those places. It is easy to overlook when things are calm, but when tensions rise, energy markets tend to react quickly. I am not a geopolitical expert, and I am not trying to predict how events unfold. Like most people, I would prefer stability. But markets do not wait for certainty. They price risk as it develops.

Right now, the situation does not need to escalate dramatically to matter. Even small changes, such as higher shipping insurance, slower transit, or cautious export behavior, can push energy costs higher. Once energy starts moving, it rarely stays contained. It tends to work its way through supply chains. One of the first places that impact shows up is fertilizer, and from there it often moves into agriculture. That is the part of the market I am focused on.

Key Market Insight

Energy disruptions rarely stop at energy. They tend to move through the system in stages. First, energy prices adjust. After that, input-heavy industries such as fertilizer production begin to respond. Eventually, those higher costs reach agricultural markets.

A similar pattern played out during 2021 and 2022. Fertilizer prices moved sharply higher, and stocks such as CF and NTR began to re-rate before earnings fully reflected those changes. Investors often focus on the headline commodity. In my view, the more interesting opportunities can appear one or two steps further down the chain.

Investment Thesis

I am not assuming an extreme outcome. What I am looking at is a period where energy markets remain somewhat tight. It does not need to be severe. A modest amount of friction is enough to keep costs elevated. When that happens, fertilizer pricing tends to follow natural gas. As fertilizer becomes more expensive, farmers adjust their usage. That can influence yields over time.

From there, the chain reaction is fairly consistent. Energy feeds into fertilizer, and fertilizer feeds into agriculture. Instead of trying to forecast oil prices directly, I prefer focusing on how those cost changes move through the system. That is why I am looking at DBA for broad exposure, SOYB options for more upside potential, and fertilizer producers such as CF, NTR, and IPI for more direct leverage.

This is not about precision. It is about positioning around a pattern that has shown up before.

Why Hormuz Matters

The importance of the strait comes down to scale. A meaningful share of global oil and LNG flows through that route. That alone makes it sensitive. What matters more for markets is how risk translates into cost. Shipping insurance can rise. Transit times can lengthen. Export behavior can become more cautious. Even modest disruptions can increase the marginal cost of supply. That is often enough to move prices across energy markets.

Historical Precedent: 2022 Shock

During the 2021–2022 energy shock, natural gas prices moved sharply higher. For example, Henry Hub natural gas rose from roughly $3 per MMBtu in 2020 to above $9 per MMBtu at peak levels in 2022 (source: U.S. Energy Information Administration).

Fertilizer prices followed and, in many cases, moved more aggressively. Urea prices increased from roughly $250 per ton to around $900 per ton, while ammonia reached levels above $1,400 per ton during the same period (source: World Bank commodity data and USDA estimates).

Agricultural commodities responded with a lag, but the direction was consistent as higher input costs worked their way into crop pricing.

What stands out here is not just the move itself, but how fertilizer pricing amplified the energy move. That is an important part of the setup because it shows how pricing power can expand when supply tightens. Intrepid Potash saw a significantly larger percentage move compared to larger peers, reflecting its smaller size and higher sensitivity to fertilizer pricing during the cycle. It is worth noting that IPI also gave back a portion of those gains as fertilizer prices normalized, highlighting the volatility of smaller-cap fertilizer names.

Natural Gas Vs. Nitrogen Fertilizer Prices (2020–2022)

Transmission Mechanism

The connection between these markets is fairly straightforward. Natural gas is the primary input for nitrogen fertilizers. When gas prices rise, fertilizer costs tend to follow. Farmers then decide how much to apply based on those costs, and that decision can influence yields.

The process is not immediate, but the direction tends to be consistent over time.

Position 1: Broad Agriculture Exposure—Invesco DB Agriculture Fund (DBA)

DBA tracks a basket of agricultural commodities, including corn, soybeans, wheat, and others. What I like about it is the diversification. Instead of trying to pick the one crop that might move the most, you’re getting exposure across the broader agricultural space. In a setup like this, that matters. DBA gives you a relatively steady way to participate if agricultural prices start moving higher. It’s generally less volatile than single-commodity ETFs, and it works well as a base position rather than something you have to trade around actively.

Compared to SOYB, the difference is fairly straightforward. DBA spreads your exposure across multiple crops, while SOYB is more focused and tends to move more directly with soybeans. I tend to think of DBA as the anchor, the core of the theme, while more targeted positions like SOYB can be layered on top.

Looking at recent performance, DBA has delivered moderate but steady returns compared to broader equity markets. Over the past year, returns have generally lagged the S&P 500 (SPY) during strong equity rallies but have held up better during periods of commodity strength and inflation pressure (source: Invesco fund data and Yahoo Finance).

Compared to a benchmark like SPY, DBA tends to behave differently. It is less tied to equity earnings growth and more influenced by commodity pricing cycles. That makes it useful as a diversification tool, especially in environments where input costs and supply constraints become more important drivers.

DBA vs. SPY Performance (5Y)

Position 2: Soybeans—Teucrium Soybean Fund (SOYB) October Calls

Rather than holding SOYB outright, I prefer using call options to express this view with defined risk. For example, a call option with a strike price around the current level, such as a $23 strike, gives exposure to moderate upside if soybean prices increase. A higher strike, such as $25, requires a larger move but offers greater leverage. Further out strikes, such as $27, are more dependent on a strong price move but can provide significant upside if that scenario develops.

The expiration matters as well. I prefer October expirations because they align with the agricultural cycle and allow time for input cost pressures to work through planting and yield expectations.

In practical terms, closer strikes provide a higher probability of success, while further strikes offer more convex returns if the move is stronger.

DBA vs. SOYB Comparison

When I look at DBA and SOYB together, I don’t really see them as competing ideas, as they serve different roles. DBA gives you broader exposure across crops, which makes it a more stable way to participate if agricultural prices move higher. It doesn’t rely on any single commodity getting the call right.

SOYB is more focused. It’s more sensitive to soybean-specific dynamics, which means it can move more if that particular market tightens. I tend to use DBA as the base layer of the idea and then use something like SOYB to add a bit more directional exposure on top.

Position 3: Fertilizer Equities

Fertilizer companies tend to respond differently from the underlying commodities. Names such as CF, NTR, and IPI often begin to move before pricing fully shows up in earnings. The market anticipates margin expansion and adjusts valuations accordingly.

What stands out is the sensitivity to pricing. When fertilizer prices are stable, these companies often trade at modest multiples due to cyclical earnings. When pricing improves, margins can expand quickly, and stocks tend to re-rate.

CF offers direct exposure to nitrogen pricing. NTR provides a more balanced profile through diversification. IPI is smaller and more volatile, which means it can move more sharply when conditions change. These moves are not always smooth, but historically, they have been meaningful when fertilizer markets tighten.

These valuations reflect the cyclical nature of these businesses. Multiples tend to stay contained during stable periods, but the earnings impact of pricing changes can be significant, which is why stocks often move ahead of fundamentals.

Scenario Analysis

I do not view these scenarios as precise forecasts. They are a way to think about how different parts of the market might respond under varying energy conditions.

At lower oil levels, the impact is likely to remain contained. As prices move higher, pressure on fertilizer costs becomes more visible, and that is where equities in that space tend to respond more strongly.

Agriculture usually reacts more gradually. It benefits from higher input costs, but the impact tends to show up with some delay and with less volatility.

Risks

No investment thesis survives contact with reality unless you talk honestly about what can go wrong. The biggest risk here is simple: the situation could cool off quickly. If tensions ease and shipping routes stabilize, energy prices would likely fall back, and a lot of the risk premium in fertilizer and agriculture markets could disappear just as fast as it arrived.

Another risk is the broader economy. If global growth slows significantly, demand for energy and agricultural commodities could weaken, which would limit upside even if supply risks remain.

Finally, commodity trades are rarely smooth rides. These markets tend to move in bursts: sharp rallies followed by equally sharp pullbacks. Anyone positioning around this theme should expect volatility along the way. In other words, this is not a “set it and forget it” type of trade.

Conclusion

Energy tends to get most of the attention in situations like this. That makes sense. It is the most visible part of the story. But the effects of energy pricing do not stop there. They move through fertilizer and into agriculture, often creating opportunities further down the chain.

We have seen this dynamic before. If energy risk remains elevated for a period of time, fertilizers and agriculture are where I would expect some of that impact to show up.
